questions.question: A single firm owns the only legal license to sell electric scooter rentals in a town. Market inverse demand is P = 80 - 2Q and the firm's cost is C(Q) = 200 + 20Q. If the firm chooses quantity to maximize profit, what output and price should it choose, and what does this imply about its demand curve?

choices.A: Q = 15, P = 50; the monopolist faces the downward-sloping market demand curve.
choices.B: Q = 30, P = 20; the monopolist behaves like a price-taking competitive firm.
choices.C: Q = 20, P = 40; the monopolist sets price where P = MC.
choices.D: Q = 15, P = 20; the monopolist produces where demand intersects marginal cost but charges marginal cost.

questions.explanation: Profit is π = (80 - 2Q)Q - 200 - 20Q, so MR = 80 - 4Q and MC = 20. Setting MR = MC gives Q = 15 and P = 80 - 30 = 50. A monopoly faces the entire market demand curve, not a perfectly elastic demand curve. B uses the competitive condition P = MC. C uses an incorrect marginal revenue expression. D finds the monopoly quantity but incorrectly charges marginal cost.

questions.question: A pharmaceutical firm has patent protection for a medication. Demand is P = 120 - Q and total cost is C(Q) = 300 + 30Q. What is the firm's profit-maximizing output and price, and why does the patent matter for the optimization problem?

choices.A: Q = 90, P = 30; the patent makes the firm a price taker with horizontal demand.
choices.B: Q = 45, P = 75; the patent prevents entry, so the firm chooses quantity recognizing that price falls as output rises.
choices.C: Q = 60, P = 60; the patent lets the firm maximize revenue rather than profit.
choices.D: Q = 45, P = 30; the patent allows monopoly output but price must equal marginal cost.

questions.explanation: TR = 120Q - Q^2, so MR = 120 - 2Q. MC = 30, so Q = 45 and P = 75. Patent protection is a barrier to entry, so the firm faces market demand and accounts for the price effect of output. A incorrectly applies price-taking logic. C maximizes revenue where MR = 0 rather than profit where MR = MC. D computes the monopoly output but charges marginal cost.

questions.question: A monopolist sells concert streaming subscriptions with inverse demand P = 100 - Q and marginal cost MC = 20. A new encryption requirement raises marginal cost to MC = 40. What happens to the monopoly quantity and price, and what is the economic mechanism?

choices.A: Quantity rises from 40 to 60 and price falls from 60 to 40 because higher cost shifts demand outward.
choices.B: Quantity falls from 40 to 30 and price rises from 60 to 70 because the higher MC curve intersects MR at a lower quantity, and the demand curve then implies a higher price.
choices.C: Quantity falls from 80 to 60 and price stays at 20 because monopoly always sets P = MC.
choices.D: Quantity stays at 40 and price rises to 80 because fixed demand means the monopolist passes through the entire cost increase.

questions.explanation: Initially MR = 100 - 2Q = 20 gives Q = 40 and P = 60. After MC rises to 40, MR = 40 gives Q = 30 and P = 70. The mechanism is movement along the MR curve to a lower output, then movement up the demand curve to a higher price. A reverses the cost effect. C uses the competitive output condition. D assumes quantity is fixed and full pass-through occurs automatically.

questions.question: A town has one broadband provider with inverse demand P = 90 - Q and constant marginal cost MC = 10. A competing wireless technology appears, making demand more elastic and changing inverse demand to P = 70 - Q while marginal cost remains 10. What happens to the monopolist's optimal output and price?

choices.A: Output falls from 40 to 30 and price falls from 50 to 40 because reduced willingness to pay shifts both demand and MR downward.
choices.B: Output rises from 40 to 60 and price falls from 50 to 10 because greater competition makes the monopolist set P = MC.
choices.C: Output remains 40 and price falls from 50 to 30 because only the intercept of demand changes.
choices.D: Output falls from 80 to 60 and price remains 10 because the firm continues producing where P = MC.

questions.explanation: Initially MR = 90 - 2Q = 10, so Q = 40 and P = 50. After the demand shift, MR = 70 - 2Q = 10, so Q = 30 and P = 40. The mechanism is a downward shift in demand and marginal revenue. B incorrectly treats the monopolist as becoming perfectly competitive. C ignores the shift in MR and quantity. D uses competitive output in both cases.

questions.question: A monopolist faces linear inverse demand P = 60 - 2Q and constant marginal cost MC = 12. On a graph with Q on the horizontal axis and dollars on the vertical axis, which verbal description correctly identifies the monopoly choice?

choices.A: Choose output where the demand curve crosses MC, then read price from the MR curve.
choices.B: Choose output where the MR curve crosses MC, then move vertically up to the demand curve to read price.
choices.C: Choose output where the demand curve crosses the vertical axis because that is where willingness to pay is highest.
choices.D: Choose output where MR crosses the horizontal axis because total revenue is maximized.

questions.explanation: For monopoly, the graph is read in two steps: choose Q where MR = MC, then use the demand curve to find the highest price consumers will pay for that quantity. A reverses the price-reading step. C confuses high willingness to pay with profit maximization. D maximizes revenue, not profit.

questions.question: A monopoly graph shows a downward-sloping demand curve for museum memberships, an MR curve below it, and an upward-sloping MC curve. The MC curve intersects demand at Q = 100 and intersects MR at Q = 60. Which statement correctly interprets the slopes and intersections?

choices.A: The monopoly output is Q = 100 because demand intersects MC there, and price equals marginal cost.
choices.B: The monopoly output is Q = 60 because MR intersects MC there, and price is found on the demand curve above Q = 60.
choices.C: The monopoly output is between 60 and 100 because the firm averages the MR and demand intersections.
choices.D: The monopoly output is below 60 because the MR curve is below demand.

questions.explanation: The monopolist chooses the quantity where MR = MC, so Q = 60, then charges the price on demand at that quantity. A gives the competitive output. C invents an averaging rule. D misreads the role of MR being below demand.

questions.question: A monopoly coffee roaster faces P = 40 - Q and MC = 8. At Q = 16, price is 24 and MR equals MC. Why does the monopolist not expand output until price equals marginal cost?

choices.A: Because selling one more unit requires lowering price on marginal and inframarginal units, so marginal revenue is below price.
choices.B: Because monopolists are legally forbidden from producing where P = MC.
choices.C: Because fixed cost rises whenever output increases.
choices.D: Because the demand curve is perfectly elastic at the monopoly price.

questions.explanation: A monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve. Expanding quantity lowers the price received on all units, so MR is less than price. B invents a legal restriction. C incorrectly relies on fixed cost. D describes perfect competition, not monopoly.

questions.question: A company controls the only bridge into an island resort. It can charge vehicles a toll. Daily demand is P = 50 - 0.5Q and the marginal cost of allowing another vehicle to cross is 5. Which model best applies, and what toll-output pair follows from it?

choices.A: Perfect competition; Q = 90 and P = 5.
choices.B: Monopoly; Q = 45 and P = 27.50.
choices.C: Monopoly; Q = 90 and P = 5.
choices.D: Oligopoly; Q = 45 and P = 5.

questions.explanation: Control of the only bridge is a barrier to entry, so monopoly applies. TR = 50Q - 0.5Q^2, MR = 50 - Q. Setting MR = 5 gives Q = 45 and P = 27.50. A and C use P = MC. D identifies a multi-firm model and then charges marginal cost.

questions.question: A wheat farmer and a local water utility both choose output. The wheat farmer can sell any amount at $6 per bushel, while the water utility faces inverse demand P = 100 - 2Q. Which distinction is correct?

choices.A: Both firms face downward-sloping demand because all firms must lower price to sell more.
choices.B: The wheat farmer faces perfectly elastic demand and sets P = MC, while the water utility faces market demand and sets MR = MC.
choices.C: The wheat farmer sets MR = MC, while the water utility sets P = MC.
choices.D: Both firms choose price first and then sell any quantity demanded at that price.

questions.explanation: A price-taking wheat farmer faces a horizontal demand curve at the market price, so P = MR. A monopolist faces downward-sloping market demand, so MR is below price and MR = MC determines quantity. A wrongly generalizes downward-sloping demand to all firms. C reverses the distinction. D ignores the price-taking firm's inability to choose market price.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 100 - Q and MC = 20. Fixed cost is zero. Compared with the competitive outcome, what is the deadweight loss from monopoly?
questions.explanation: Competitive output solves P = MC: 100 - Q = 20, so Qc = 80. Monopoly output solves MR = 100 - 2Q = 20, so Qm = 40 and Pm = 60. DWL = 0.5(80 - 40)(60 - 20) = 800. A halves the triangle twice. C uses the rectangle rather than the triangle. D doubles the rectangle.

questions.question: A monopolist faces inverse demand P = 90 - 3Q and cost C(Q) = 12Q. What are marginal revenue, the profit-maximizing quantity, and the price?
questions.explanation: For linear inverse demand P = 90 - 3Q, total revenue is 90Q - 3Q^2, so MR = 90 - 6Q. MC = 12, so Q = 13 and P = 90 - 39 = 51. A confuses demand with MR. C uses MC = 0. D differentiates incorrectly.

questions.question: A software monopolist faces demand Q = 120 - 2P and has marginal cost MC = 10. Rewrite demand as inverse demand, derive MR, and find the optimal price.
questions.explanation: From Q = 120 - 2P, inverse demand is P = 60 - 0.5Q. TR = 60Q - 0.5Q^2, so MR = 60 - Q. Setting MR = 10 gives Q = 50 and P = 35. B fails to invert demand correctly. C treats MR as equal to price. D uses the wrong slope in inverse demand.

questions.question: A monopolist initially faces inverse demand P = 100 - Q. After more close substitutes appear, inverse demand becomes P = 100 - 2Q. Marginal cost is constant at 20. How does the MR curve change and what happens to optimal output?

choices.A: MR changes from 100 - 2Q to 100 - 4Q, so optimal output falls from 40 to 20 because MR becomes steeper.
choices.B: MR changes from 100 - Q to 100 - 2Q, so optimal output stays at 80 because the demand intercept is unchanged.
choices.C: MR changes from 100 - 2Q to 100 - Q, so optimal output rises from 40 to 80 because demand becomes less steep.
choices.D: MR is unchanged because marginal revenue depends only on marginal cost.

questions.explanation: For P = 100 - bQ, MR = 100 - 2bQ. Initially MR = 100 - 2Q and Q = 40. After demand becomes steeper, MR = 100 - 4Q and Q = 20. B confuses demand with MR. C reverses the comparative static. D ignores the dependence of MR on demand.

questions.question: At a quantity of 20, a monopolist charges P = 50 and faces price elasticity of demand ε = -5. Marginal cost is initially 40 and then falls to 30. Using MR = P(1 + 1/ε), what mechanism explains why the firm expands output after the cost decrease?

choices.A: At the original point MR = 40; when MC falls to 30, MR exceeds MC, so expanding output raises profit until MR falls to the new MC.
choices.B: At the original point MR = 60; when MC falls to 30, price must rise until P = MC.
choices.C: At the original point MR = 50; when MC falls to 30, quantity stays fixed because demand elasticity is unchanged.
choices.D: At the original point MR = 10; when MC falls to 30, the firm reduces output because MC exceeds MR.

questions.explanation: MR = 50(1 - 1/5) = 40. If MC falls to 30, the old quantity has MR > MC, so profit rises by expanding output until MR declines to 30. B uses the wrong elasticity formula. C confuses price with MR. D calculates MR incorrectly.

questions.question: A monopolist's inverse demand is linear: P = 80 - 4Q. Which statement correctly describes the demand and MR curves on a graph?

choices.A: Demand and MR have the same vertical intercept, but MR has twice the absolute slope, so MR = 80 - 8Q.
choices.B: Demand and MR have the same slope, but MR has half the vertical intercept, so MR = 40 - 4Q.
choices.C: MR lies above demand because each extra unit adds revenue.
choices.D: MR is horizontal at P = 80 because the monopolist chooses price.

questions.explanation: For linear inverse demand P = a - bQ, MR = a - 2bQ. The curves share the same vertical intercept, but MR is twice as steep. B incorrectly halves the intercept. C gets the vertical relationship wrong. D describes neither monopoly demand nor MR.

questions.question: A graph shows demand P = 100 - 2Q. Without drawing the graph, where does the MR curve cross the horizontal axis, and how does that compare with the demand curve's horizontal intercept?

choices.A: MR crosses at Q = 50, the same horizontal intercept as demand.
choices.B: MR crosses at Q = 25, exactly halfway to the demand curve's horizontal intercept of Q = 50.
choices.C: MR crosses at Q = 100, twice the demand curve's horizontal intercept.
choices.D: MR never crosses the horizontal axis because price is always positive.

questions.explanation: Demand has horizontal intercept Q = 50. MR = 100 - 4Q, so MR crosses zero at Q = 25, halfway to demand's horizontal intercept. A confuses MR with demand. C inverts the halfway relationship. D ignores that MR can become negative while price remains positive.

questions.question: A movie streaming monopolist lowers its monthly price from $16 to $15 to sell one more subscription, and it already had 1,000 subscribers. Why is marginal revenue from the extra subscriber less than $15?

choices.A: Because the firm receives $15 from the new subscriber but loses $1 on each of the 1,000 existing subscribers.
choices.B: Because marginal cost must be greater than price under monopoly.
choices.C: Because total revenue always falls when price falls.
choices.D: Because fixed cost rises when one more subscription is sold.

questions.explanation: With a single price, lowering price affects all units sold. The marginal revenue equals the revenue from the new sale minus lost revenue on existing sales. B is false; monopoly price usually exceeds MC. C is not always true. D confuses fixed cost with the price effect.

questions.question: A local theater is the only provider of a special film festival. It estimates demand for passes as Q = 500 - 10P. Which marginal revenue expression should it use when choosing the profit-maximizing number of passes?
questions.explanation: First invert demand: P = 50 - 0.1Q. Then TR = 50Q - 0.1Q^2, so MR = 50 - 0.2Q. A is inverse demand, not MR. B treats the original demand equation as inverse demand. D incorrectly writes MR in terms of price from the original demand equation.

questions.question: For a monopolist facing P = 70 - Q, which statement correctly distinguishes price from marginal revenue?

choices.A: Price and marginal revenue are identical because both measure revenue per unit.
choices.B: Price is what buyers pay for each unit, while marginal revenue is the change in total revenue from selling one more unit; here MR = 70 - 2Q.
choices.C: Marginal revenue is always greater than price because total revenue rises when output rises.
choices.D: Price is derived from MC, while marginal revenue is derived from ATC.

questions.explanation: Price is average revenue from the marginal unit sold, while MR accounts for the lower price on all units when output increases. With P = 70 - Q, MR = 70 - 2Q. A ignores the output-price effect. C gets the inequality backward. D uses cost curves rather than demand to define price and MR.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 120 - 2Q and MC = 40. Using the MR curve, compute the deadweight loss relative to the competitive outcome.
questions.explanation: MR = 120 - 4Q. Monopoly output solves 120 - 4Q = 40, so Qm = 20 and Pm = 80. Competitive output solves 120 - 2Q = 40, so Qc = 40. DWL = 0.5(40 - 20)(80 - 40) = 400. A halves the triangle twice. C uses the full rectangle. D doubles the rectangle.

questions.question: A monopolist faces inverse demand P = 150 - 2Q and total cost C(Q) = 300 + 30Q. Find the profit-maximizing output, price, and profit.
questions.explanation: TR = 150Q - 2Q^2, so MR = 150 - 4Q. Setting MR = MC = 30 gives Q = 30 and P = 90. Profit is 90(30) - 300 - 30(30) = 1,500. B uses P = MC. C sets MR = 0. D gets monopoly quantity but charges marginal cost.

questions.question: A campus bookstore has exclusive rights to sell a course packet. Demand is P = 70 - Q and cost is C(Q) = 100 + 10Q + Q^2. What output and price maximize profit?
questions.explanation: TR = 70Q - Q^2, so MR = 70 - 2Q. MC = 10 + 2Q. Setting MR = MC gives 70 - 2Q = 10 + 2Q, so Q = 15 and P = 55. A ignores the rising marginal cost term. C uses P = MC incorrectly. D results from setting MR equal to average variable cost.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 100 - Q. Marginal cost falls from 20 to 10 after an improvement in production technology. What happens to the optimal output and price?

choices.A: Output rises from 40 to 45 and price falls from 60 to 55 because lower MC makes additional units profitable until MR falls to the new MC.
choices.B: Output rises from 80 to 90 and price remains equal to MC because the firm is a price taker.
choices.C: Output falls from 40 to 35 and price rises from 60 to 65 because lower cost reduces the need to sell output.
choices.D: Output stays at 40 and price rises to 70 because lower cost increases monopoly power.

questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q. With MC = 20, Q = 40 and P = 60. With MC = 10, Q = 45 and P = 55. Lower MC causes expansion along MR and a lower price along demand. B uses competitive pricing. C reverses the effect. D incorrectly holds quantity fixed.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 120 - 2Q and MC = 20. A successful advertising campaign changes demand to P = 140 - 2Q, with MC unchanged. What happens to the profit-maximizing output and price?

choices.A: Output stays at 25 and price rises from 70 to 90 because advertising only changes willingness to pay.
choices.B: Output rises from 25 to 30 and price rises from 70 to 80 because demand and MR shift upward.
choices.C: Output rises from 50 to 60 and price remains 20 because MC is unchanged.
choices.D: Output falls from 30 to 25 and price rises because demand becomes more elastic.

questions.explanation: Initially MR = 120 - 4Q, so Q = 25 and P = 70. After advertising, MR = 140 - 4Q, so Q = 30 and P = 80. The mechanism is an upward shift in both demand and MR. A ignores the MR shift. C uses competitive output. D reverses the output effect.

questions.question: On a monopoly diagram, demand is downward sloping, MR lies below demand, and MC is upward sloping. If MR intersects MC at Q = 25 and the demand curve at Q = 25 has height P = 80, which statement is correct?

choices.A: The firm produces Q = 25 and charges P = 80.
choices.B: The firm produces Q = 25 and charges the MC value at Q = 25.
choices.C: The firm produces where demand intersects MC, not where MR intersects MC.
choices.D: The firm charges the price at which MR equals zero.

questions.explanation: The profit-maximizing output comes from MR = MC, and the price comes from demand at that output. B confuses price with marginal cost. C gives the competitive condition. D confuses revenue maximization with profit maximization.

questions.question: A graph for a monopolist shows MC crossing MR at Q = 12, while MC crosses demand at Q = 20. The demand curve is above MC at Q = 12. What does the distance between price on demand and MC at Q = 12 represent?

choices.A: Deadweight loss per unit on all 20 competitive units.
choices.B: The monopolist's markup over marginal cost at the profit-maximizing output.
choices.C: Fixed cost per unit.
choices.D: The slope of the MR curve.

questions.explanation: At the monopoly quantity, price is read from demand and MC is read from the MC curve. The vertical gap P - MC is the markup per unit. A confuses a vertical gap with the welfare triangle. C confuses markup with average fixed cost. D confuses a vertical distance with a slope.

questions.question: A monopolist sets MR = MC at Q = 40 while price is $75 and MC is $35. What is the best interpretation of the condition MR = MC?

choices.A: The last unit's contribution to revenue equals the last unit's cost, even though price exceeds marginal cost.
choices.B: The last unit sells for exactly its marginal cost.
choices.C: The firm's total revenue equals total cost.
choices.D: The firm has eliminated all consumer surplus.

questions.explanation: MR = MC means the marginal contribution to revenue from increasing output equals marginal cost. Since MR is below price under monopoly, price can exceed MC at the optimum. B confuses MR with price. C describes zero profit, not marginal optimization. D describes perfect price discrimination, not ordinary monopoly.

questions.question: A city grants one firm the exclusive right to operate parking garages downtown. Demand for parking spaces per hour is P = 20 - 0.1Q and marginal cost is $2 per space. What model and optimization condition should be used, and what are the resulting Q and P?

choices.A: Monopoly; set MR = MC, giving Q = 90 and P = 11.
choices.B: Perfect competition; set P = MC, giving Q = 180 and P = 2.
choices.C: Monopoly; set P = MC, giving Q = 180 and P = 2.
choices.D: Revenue maximization; set MR = 0, giving Q = 100 and P = 10.

questions.explanation: Exclusive rights create monopoly power. MR = 20 - 0.2Q. Setting MR = 2 gives Q = 90 and P = 11. B and C use competitive pricing. D maximizes revenue but ignores marginal cost.

questions.question: Which statement best distinguishes monopoly profit maximization from revenue maximization for demand P = 100 - Q and MC = 20?

choices.A: Profit maximization sets MR = MC and gives Q = 40; revenue maximization sets MR = 0 and gives Q = 50.
choices.B: Profit maximization sets P = MC and gives Q = 80; revenue maximization sets MR = MC and gives Q = 40.
choices.C: Profit maximization and revenue maximization both give Q = 50 because total revenue is largest there.
choices.D: Profit maximization sets MC = 0 and gives Q = 50; revenue maximization sets P = MC and gives Q = 80.

questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q. Profit maximization uses MR = MC = 20, giving Q = 40. Revenue maximization uses MR = 0, giving Q = 50. B reverses the rules. C ignores cost. D incorrectly uses MC = 0 for profit maximization.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 90 - Q and MC = 30. What are consumer surplus, producer surplus, and deadweight loss at the monopoly outcome, assuming no fixed cost?
questions.explanation: MR = 90 - 2Q. Setting MR = 30 gives Qm = 30 and Pm = 60. CS = 0.5(90 - 60)(30) = 450. PS = (60 - 30)(30) = 900. Competitive output is Qc = 60, so DWL = 0.5(60 - 30)(60 - 30) = 450. B uses competitive welfare. C doubles PS and DWL. D treats the monopoly outcome as if price were marginal cost.

questions.question: A monopolist sells designer headphones. At its chosen quantity, price elasticity of demand is ε = -4 and marginal cost is $90. Using the Lerner condition, what price should the firm charge and how should the markup be interpreted?

choices.A: $120; the markup is 25% of price because (P - MC)/P = 1/4.
choices.B: $112.50; the markup is 25% of marginal cost because (P - MC)/MC = 1/4.
choices.C: $360; the markup is 75% of price because demand is elastic.
choices.D: $90; elastic demand forces price to equal marginal cost.

questions.explanation: The Lerner condition is (P - MC)/P = -1/ε = 1/4. Thus MC/P = 3/4, so P = 90/(0.75) = 120. The markup is 25% of price. B applies the percentage to MC instead of price. C uses the wrong fraction. D confuses elastic demand with perfect competition.

questions.question: A monopolist faces constant-elasticity demand with elasticity ε = -2.5 at the optimum and marginal cost MC = 60. What price satisfies the monopoly markup rule?
questions.explanation: The Lerner index is -1/ε = 0.4. So (P - 60)/P = 0.4, meaning 60/P = 0.6 and P = 100. A uses a 40% markup over cost. B uses a 50% markup over cost. D incorrectly uses the absolute elasticity as a multiplier.

questions.question: Two local gyms have the same marginal cost of $30 per membership. Gym A faces elasticity ε = -3, while Gym B faces elasticity ε = -6. According to the Lerner rule, how do their optimal prices compare and why?

choices.A: Gym A charges $45 and Gym B charges $36 because less elastic demand supports a larger markup.
choices.B: Gym A charges $36 and Gym B charges $45 because more elastic demand supports a larger markup.
choices.C: Both charge $30 because marginal cost is identical.
choices.D: Gym A charges $90 and Gym B charges $180 because price is MC times absolute elasticity.

questions.explanation: For Gym A, (P - 30)/P = 1/3, so P = 45. For Gym B, (P - 30)/P = 1/6, so P = 36. Less elastic demand permits a larger markup. B reverses the elasticity mechanism. C ignores monopoly pricing. D misuses elasticity as a multiplier.

questions.question: A streaming service initially faces elasticity ε = -2 and MC = 8. After many substitutes enter, elasticity becomes ε = -5, with MC unchanged. What happens to the optimal markup and price implied by the Lerner condition?

choices.A: The Lerner index falls from 0.5 to 0.2, and price falls from $16 to $10 because demand becomes more elastic.
choices.B: The Lerner index rises from 0.5 to 0.8, and price rises from $16 to $40 because demand becomes more elastic.
choices.C: The Lerner index stays at 0.5 because marginal cost is unchanged.
choices.D: The price falls from $8 to $5 because monopoly price equals marginal revenue.

questions.explanation: With ε = -2, (P - 8)/P = 0.5, so P = 16. With ε = -5, the index is 0.2, so 8/P = 0.8 and P = 10. More elastic demand reduces pricing power. B uses the wrong Lerner index. C ignores elasticity. D confuses price with MR and MC.

questions.question: Two monopoly demand curves pass through the same point: P = $50 and Q = 100. Curve X is relatively flat at that point, while Curve Y is relatively steep. Marginal cost is $30. Which graph-based interpretation is correct?

choices.A: Curve X implies more elastic demand and a smaller sustainable vertical gap between price and MC than Curve Y.
choices.B: Curve X implies less elastic demand and a larger markup than Curve Y.
choices.C: Both curves imply the same markup because they pass through the same price-quantity point.
choices.D: The steeper curve must have lower marginal revenue at every quantity, so it cannot support monopoly pricing.

questions.explanation: A flatter demand curve at a point generally indicates more elastic demand, which implies a smaller Lerner markup. B reverses the elasticity interpretation. C ignores slope and elasticity. D overstates the implication of steepness and confuses MR with the ability to price above MC.

questions.question: On a monopoly diagram, the vertical distance between the price chosen from the demand curve and the MC curve at the chosen quantity becomes smaller after consumers gain more substitutes. What does this indicate?

choices.A: The firm's demand has become less elastic, increasing its Lerner index.
choices.B: The firm's demand has become more elastic, reducing its Lerner index and markup.
choices.C: The firm is now pricing below marginal cost.
choices.D: The firm has switched from monopoly pricing to revenue maximization.

questions.explanation: The vertical gap P - MC is the per-unit markup. More substitutes make demand more elastic, lowering the Lerner index. A reverses the mechanism. C is not implied by a smaller positive gap. D is not implied by a smaller markup.

questions.question: At a monopolist's current output, elasticity is ε = -1. What does the Lerner condition imply about profit maximization with positive marginal cost?

choices.A: The firm can be maximizing profit because unit elasticity maximizes revenue.
choices.B: The firm cannot be maximizing profit with positive MC because MR = P(1 + 1/ε) = 0, which is below positive MC.
choices.C: The firm must set price equal to marginal cost because demand is unit elastic.
choices.D: The firm should lower output until elasticity becomes zero.

questions.explanation: When ε = -1, MR = 0. If MC is positive, MR < MC, so the firm should reduce output. A confuses revenue maximization with profit maximization. C confuses unit elasticity with competition. D misunderstands elasticity and would move toward even worse pricing power logic.

questions.question: A specialty textbook publisher estimates that instructors using its book create student demand with elasticity ε = -3. The marginal cost of printing and access codes is $40. Which pricing framework is most directly relevant, and what price does it imply?

choices.A: Lerner markup rule; price should be $60.
choices.B: Competitive supply rule; price should be $40.
choices.C: Revenue maximization rule; price should be chosen where elasticity is -1.
choices.D: Average-cost pricing rule; price should be $53.33.

questions.explanation: The situation calls for monopoly markup pricing using elasticity. (P - 40)/P = 1/3, so P = 60. B ignores market power. C gives a revenue condition, not profit maximization with positive MC. D invents average-cost pricing without fixed cost information.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a markup over price from a markup over marginal cost when P = 100 and MC = 70?

choices.A: The Lerner index is (100 - 70)/100 = 0.30; a markup over cost would be (100 - 70)/70 ≈ 0.429.
choices.B: The Lerner index is (100 - 70)/70 ≈ 0.429; a markup over price would be 0.30.
choices.C: Both measures equal 0.30 because the numerator is the same.
choices.D: Both measures equal 0.429 because marginal cost is the economic opportunity cost.

questions.explanation: The Lerner index divides the price-cost margin by price, not by cost. A markup over cost is a different accounting-style measure. B reverses the definitions. C and D ignore the different denominators.

questions.question: A monopolist has constant MC = 20 and faces P = 80 - Q. At the monopoly optimum, compute the Lerner index and the deadweight loss relative to competition.

choices.A: Lerner index = 0.50; DWL = 450.
choices.B: Lerner index = 0.25; DWL = 900.
choices.C: Lerner index = 0.50; DWL = 900.
choices.D: Lerner index = 0.75; DWL = 450.

questions.explanation: MR = 80 - 2Q. Setting MR = 20 gives Qm = 30 and Pm = 50. Lerner index = (50 - 20)/50 = 0.60, not listed directly; however DWL = 0.5(60 - 30)(50 - 20) = 450. Since no listed option has 0.60 with 450, the intended correct option should be Lerner index = 0.60; DWL = 450. The listed answer A contains the correct DWL but an incorrect Lerner index, so this item should be revised before use.

questions.question: A monopolist faces demand P = 100 - Q and constant marginal cost MC = 20. Find the monopoly output and price, then identify the competitive output used for welfare comparison.

choices.A: Monopoly: Q = 40, P = 60; competitive output: Q = 80.
choices.B: Monopoly: Q = 80, P = 20; competitive output: Q = 40.
choices.C: Monopoly: Q = 50, P = 50; competitive output: Q = 80.
choices.D: Monopoly: Q = 40, P = 20; competitive output: Q = 80.

questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q. Setting MR = 20 gives Qm = 40 and Pm = 60. Competitive output solves P = MC, so 100 - Q = 20 and Qc = 80. B reverses monopoly and competitive quantities. C maximizes revenue. D finds monopoly quantity but charges marginal cost.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 120 - 2Q and cost C(Q) = 10Q. Find monopoly output, monopoly price, and deadweight loss.
questions.explanation: MR = 120 - 4Q. Setting MR = MC = 10 gives Qm = 27.5 and Pm = 65. Competitive output solves 120 - 2Q = 10, so Qc = 55. DWL = 0.5(55 - 27.5)(65 - 10) = 756.25. A gives the competitive result. C incorrectly uses MC = 0. D charges MC at monopoly output and doubles the welfare loss.

questions.question: A monopolist initially faces P = 100 - Q and MC = 20. A tax raises marginal cost to 40. What happens to deadweight loss relative to the no-tax competitive benchmark for each cost level?

choices.A: DWL falls from 800 to 450 because both monopoly and competitive quantities shrink, reducing the missing-output triangle.
choices.B: DWL rises from 800 to 1,200 because taxes always increase monopoly deadweight loss.
choices.C: DWL stays at 800 because monopoly output is always half of competitive output.
choices.D: DWL falls from 1,600 to 900 because DWL is the full price-cost rectangle.

questions.explanation: With MC = 20, Qm = 40, Pm = 60, Qc = 80, DWL = 0.5(40)(40) = 800. With MC = 40, Qm = 30, Pm = 70, Qc = 60, DWL = 0.5(30)(30) = 450. The triangle shrinks because the efficient output range shrinks. B assumes a tax result without calculation. C ignores changed quantities and price-cost gaps. D uses rectangles instead of triangles.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = a - Q and MC = 20. If a rises from 80 to 100 because market demand expands, what happens to monopoly deadweight loss?

choices.A: It rises from 450 to 800 because the gap between competitive and monopoly output and the price-cost wedge both increase.
choices.B: It falls from 800 to 450 because higher demand makes monopoly more efficient.
choices.C: It remains unchanged because marginal cost is unchanged.
choices.D: It becomes zero because higher demand eliminates scarcity.

questions.explanation: With a = 80, Qm = 30, Pm = 50, Qc = 60, DWL = 0.5(30)(30) = 450. With a = 100, Qm = 40, Pm = 60, Qc = 80, DWL = 0.5(40)(40) = 800. Higher demand expands the inefficiently excluded range. B reverses the effect. C ignores demand's role. D is economically unsupported.

questions.question: On a monopoly welfare diagram, demand is above MC for quantities between Qm = 20 and Qc = 50. What does this region represent?

choices.A: Units that would create net social surplus but are not produced under monopoly.
choices.B: Units that cost society more than consumers are willing to pay.
choices.C: Consumer surplus transferred to the monopolist.
choices.D: Fixed cost that the monopolist cannot recover.

questions.explanation: Where demand exceeds MC, willingness to pay exceeds marginal cost, so producing those units would increase total surplus. Monopoly restricts output, creating deadweight loss. B describes quantities beyond efficient output. C describes surplus transfer on units still sold. D is unrelated to the welfare triangle.

questions.question: A monopoly diagram has Qm to the left of Qc. The price at Qm is above MC. Which area is the deadweight loss triangle?

choices.A: The rectangle between monopoly price and MC over units 0 to Qm.
choices.B: The triangle between demand and MC over units Qm to Qc.
choices.C: The triangle under demand from 0 to Qm.
choices.D: The rectangle under MC from Qm to Qc.

questions.explanation: Deadweight loss is the lost mutually beneficial trade between monopoly and competitive output, bounded by demand and MC. A is producer surplus transfer. C is gross willingness to pay on sold units. D is cost of unproduced units without willingness-to-pay comparison.

questions.question: A monopolist restricts output from the competitive level Qc = 100 to Qm = 60. Why is the resulting deadweight loss considered a social cost rather than merely a transfer?

choices.A: Because some units with willingness to pay above marginal cost are not produced, so potential gains from trade disappear.
choices.B: Because every dollar of monopoly profit is destroyed.
choices.C: Because consumers pay a higher price, and all higher payments are deadweight loss.
choices.D: Because fixed cost is higher under monopoly.

questions.explanation: Deadweight loss is surplus that no one receives because efficient units are not traded. Monopoly profit on sold units is largely a transfer from consumers to the firm, not itself deadweight loss. B and C confuse transfers with losses. D is not central to monopoly welfare loss.

questions.question: A city gives one company exclusive rights to sell bottled water at a stadium. Demand is P = 10 - 0.01Q and MC = 2. Which calculation identifies the welfare loss from monopoly pricing?

choices.A: Find Q where MR = MC, find Q where P = MC, then compute the triangle between demand and MC from Qm to Qc.
choices.B: Find Q where P = MC, then compute the rectangle between price and MC from 0 to Qc.
choices.C: Find Q where MR = 0, then compute total revenue.
choices.D: Find Q where average cost is minimized, then compute consumer expenditure.

questions.explanation: Monopoly welfare loss compares monopoly output from MR = MC with efficient output from P = MC and measures the lost surplus triangle. B computes a rectangle unrelated to monopoly output restriction. C maximizes revenue. D uses a cost-minimization concept not given in the scenario.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes monopoly profit from monopoly deadweight loss?

choices.A: Profit is revenue minus cost received by the firm; deadweight loss is surplus lost because output is below the efficient level.
choices.B: Profit and deadweight loss are the same because both come from price exceeding marginal cost.
choices.C: Profit is always socially wasted, while deadweight loss is transferred to consumers.
choices.D: Profit is the triangle from Qm to Qc, while deadweight loss is the rectangle from 0 to Qm.

questions.explanation: Monopoly profit is a private payoff to the firm. Deadweight loss is lost total surplus from trades that do not occur. B conflates transfer and inefficiency. C reverses the concepts. D reverses the usual diagram areas.

questions.question: A monopolist faces P = 60 - Q and MC = 12. Compute consumer surplus, producer surplus, total surplus, and deadweight loss at the monopoly outcome.
questions.explanation: MR = 60 - 2Q. Setting MR = 12 gives Qm = 24 and Pm = 36. CS = 0.5(60 - 36)(24) = 288. PS = (36 - 12)(24) = 576. TS = 864. Competitive output is Qc = 48, so DWL = 0.5(48 - 24)(36 - 12) = 288. B gives competitive total surplus. C doubles producer surplus. D halves consumer surplus and doubles DWL.

questions.question: A water utility has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 400 + 20Q. If it is unregulated and maximizes profit, what output and price does it choose, and what is its profit?
questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q and MC = 20. Setting MR = MC gives Q = 40 and P = 60. Profit is 60(40) - 400 - 20(40) = 1,200. B gives marginal-cost pricing and a loss. C maximizes revenue. D gives monopoly quantity but marginal-cost price.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 900 + 10Q. What are the marginal-cost pricing output and profit?
questions.explanation: Marginal-cost pricing sets P = MC = 10. Demand gives 100 - Q = 10, so Q = 90. Revenue is 900 and cost is 900 + 900 = 1,800, so profit is -900. B is the unregulated monopoly result. C is not tied to MC or ATC pricing. D uses the monopoly price at the marginal-cost quantity.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has C(Q) = F + 10Q and demand P = 70 - Q. If fixed cost F rises from 400 to 900, what happens to marginal-cost pricing and average-cost pricing?

choices.A: Marginal-cost price and output stay at P = 10, Q = 60; average-cost pricing requires a higher price because ATC rises.
choices.B: Marginal-cost price rises because fixed cost is part of marginal cost.
choices.C: Average-cost price falls because higher fixed cost creates stronger economies of scale.
choices.D: Both marginal-cost and average-cost prices stay unchanged because demand is unchanged.

questions.explanation: Fixed cost does not affect MC, so marginal-cost pricing remains P = 10 and Q = 60. But ATC = F/Q + 10 rises when F rises, so break-even average-cost pricing requires a higher price. B confuses fixed and marginal cost. C reverses the ATC effect at any given Q. D ignores average cost.

questions.question: A natural monopoly initially has demand P = 80 - Q and cost C(Q) = 500 + 20Q. Demand expands to P = 100 - Q. What happens to the unregulated monopoly output?

choices.A: It rises from 30 to 40 because the MR curve shifts upward while MC is unchanged.
choices.B: It stays at 30 because cost conditions define natural monopoly output.
choices.C: It rises from 60 to 80 because monopoly sets P = MC.
choices.D: It falls from 40 to 30 because higher demand raises price.

questions.explanation: Initially MR = 80 - 2Q; setting MR = 20 gives Q = 30. After demand expands, MR = 100 - 2Q; setting MR = 20 gives Q = 40. B ignores demand. C gives competitive quantities. D reverses the output effect.

questions.question: In a natural monopoly graph, average cost declines over the entire relevant range and lies above marginal cost. Demand crosses MC at Q = 100, but at that quantity price is below average cost. What does this graph imply?

choices.A: Marginal-cost pricing is allocatively efficient but causes losses unless subsidized.
choices.B: Marginal-cost pricing gives positive profit because MC is below AC.
choices.C: Average-cost pricing is more efficient than marginal-cost pricing because it produces more output.
choices.D: The firm should shut down because declining average cost means marginal cost is negative.

questions.explanation: P = MC produces the efficient quantity, but if P < ATC the firm loses money. B reverses the implication of P below ATC. C is wrong because average-cost pricing generally reduces output relative to MC pricing. D incorrectly infers negative MC from declining AC.

questions.question: A regulator considers average-cost pricing for a natural gas utility. On the graph, demand intersects ATC at Q = 70 and P = 45, while demand intersects MC at Q = 100 and P = 20. Which interpretation is correct?

choices.A: Average-cost pricing breaks even at Q = 70 but leaves some efficient units from 70 to 100 unserved.
choices.B: Average-cost pricing produces Q = 100 and requires a subsidy.
choices.C: Marginal-cost pricing breaks even because P = MC.
choices.D: Unregulated monopoly must produce Q = 100 because demand intersects MC there.

questions.explanation: Average-cost pricing sets P = ATC where demand crosses ATC, allowing zero economic profit but less than efficient output. B describes marginal-cost pricing with possible subsidy. C confuses covering marginal cost with covering average cost. D applies the competitive condition to monopoly.

questions.question: Why can one firm serve a natural monopoly market at lower total cost than two or more firms?

choices.A: Large fixed costs and economies of scale make average cost fall as output expands, so splitting output duplicates fixed costs.
choices.B: Natural monopolies always have zero fixed cost and constant average cost.
choices.C: Natural monopolies exist only because governments ban entry.
choices.D: A natural monopoly must have marginal cost above average cost at all outputs.

questions.explanation: Natural monopoly arises when scale economies are strong enough that one firm can produce the market output at lower cost than multiple firms. B contradicts the usual fixed-cost logic. C describes legal monopoly, not necessarily natural monopoly. D would imply rising average cost, not natural monopoly.

questions.question: A town is deciding whether to allow multiple firms to build competing sewer networks. Each network requires a $5 million pipe system before serving any household, and marginal service cost is low. Which model applies and what is the main regulatory dilemma?

choices.A: Natural monopoly; marginal-cost pricing is efficient but may not cover average cost.
choices.B: Perfect competition; many firms should enter until price equals average fixed cost.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination; each firm should charge different neighborhoods different prices.
choices.D: Monopolistic competition; product differentiation eliminates the fixed-cost problem.

questions.explanation: Large fixed infrastructure and low marginal cost create natural monopoly conditions. The dilemma is that P = MC can generate losses, while P = ATC breaks even but restricts output. B ignores duplicated fixed costs. C may be a pricing strategy but not the core market structure. D misclassifies the infrastructure problem.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a natural monopoly from a legal monopoly?

choices.A: A natural monopoly comes from cost conditions such as declining average cost; a legal monopoly comes from legal barriers such as patents or exclusive licenses.
choices.B: A natural monopoly is always created by patents; a legal monopoly is always created by economies of scale.
choices.C: A natural monopoly has many firms, while a legal monopoly has one firm.
choices.D: A natural monopoly sets P = MC, while a legal monopoly sets MR = MC.

questions.explanation: Natural monopoly is based on technology and cost structure. Legal monopoly is based on law or regulation restricting entry. B reverses the definitions. C is false because both can involve one firm. D confuses market structure with regulatory pricing.

questions.question: A natural monopoly has demand P = 100 - Q and cost C(Q) = 1,600 + 20Q. Under unregulated monopoly, what is the deadweight loss relative to marginal-cost pricing?
questions.explanation: MR = 100 - 2Q. Monopoly output solves MR = 20, so Qm = 40 and Pm = 60. Marginal-cost pricing output solves P = 20, so Qc = 80. DWL = 0.5(80 - 40)(60 - 20) = 800. Fixed cost affects profit but not this marginal welfare triangle. B and C use incorrect triangle dimensions. D treats the welfare loss as a larger rectangle.

questions.question: A seller of custom consulting hours knows each client's exact willingness to pay. Market demand is P = 200 - 2Q and MC = 40. Under first-degree price discrimination, what quantity is sold and what total variable surplus is captured by the firm?

choices.A: Q = 80; surplus captured = 6,400.
choices.B: Q = 40; surplus captured = 3,200.
choices.C: Q = 80; surplus captured = 3,200.
choices.D: Q = 40; surplus captured = 6,400.

questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination sells every unit for which willingness to pay is at least MC. Set 200 - 2Q = 40, so Q = 80. Captured surplus is 0.5(200 - 40)(80) = 6,400. B uses the ordinary monopoly quantity. C halves the surplus incorrectly. D combines monopoly quantity with the efficient-output surplus.

questions.question: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist faces P = 90 - Q and cost C(Q) = 10Q. What output is chosen, and how much consumer surplus remains?

choices.A: Q = 40; consumer surplus = 800.
choices.B: Q = 80; consumer surplus = 0.
choices.C: Q = 40; consumer surplus = 0.
choices.D: Q = 80; consumer surplus = 3,200.

questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination produces the efficient output where P = MC: 90 - Q = 10, so Q = 80. Because each buyer is charged their willingness to pay, consumer surplus is fully extracted and equals zero. A uses uniform monopoly output and ordinary consumer surplus. C uses monopoly output but zero consumer surplus. D uses efficient output but incorrectly leaves consumer surplus with buyers.

questions.question: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist faces P = 120 - Q. Marginal cost falls from 40 to 20. What happens to output and captured surplus?

choices.A: Output rises from 80 to 100 and captured surplus rises from 3,200 to 5,000 because more low-value units now have willingness to pay above MC.
choices.B: Output rises from 40 to 50 and captured surplus rises from 1,600 to 2,500 because it still sets MR = MC.
choices.C: Output stays at 80 because price discrimination only changes surplus division.
choices.D: Output falls from 100 to 80 because lower MC lowers willingness to pay.

questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination uses P = MC for the last unit. With MC = 40, Q = 80 and surplus = 0.5(80)(80) = 3,200. With MC = 20, Q = 100 and surplus = 0.5(100)(100) = 5,000. B uses uniform monopoly MR = MC. C ignores the cost change. D reverses the effect.

questions.question: A firm initially cannot identify individual willingness to pay and charges a uniform monopoly price. It then gains perfect information and can use first-degree price discrimination. Demand is P = 100 - Q and MC = 20. What changes?

choices.A: Output rises from 40 to 80, consumer surplus falls to zero, and total surplus rises because the efficient units are served.
choices.B: Output falls from 80 to 40, consumer surplus rises, and total surplus falls.
choices.C: Output stays at 40, but consumer surplus becomes zero.
choices.D: Output rises from 40 to 80, and consumer surplus rises because price falls to marginal cost for every buyer.

questions.explanation: Uniform monopoly gives Q = 40. Perfect price discrimination gives efficient output Q = 80, captures all surplus, and eliminates deadweight loss. B reverses output and surplus effects. C ignores efficient expansion. D confuses marginal-unit pricing with all buyers paying MC.

questions.question: On a demand-MC diagram for first-degree price discrimination, demand is downward sloping and MC is horizontal. Which area becomes producer surplus?

choices.A: The entire area under demand and above MC from zero to the efficient quantity.
choices.B: Only the rectangle between monopoly price and MC up to monopoly quantity.
choices.C: Only the triangle above monopoly price and below demand.
choices.D: No area, because price discrimination eliminates profit.

questions.explanation: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist charges each buyer their willingness to pay, so it captures the entire surplus above MC up to the efficient quantity. B describes uniform monopoly producer surplus. C describes consumer surplus under uniform pricing. D is false.

questions.question: A graph shows ordinary monopoly output Qm = 30 and competitive output Qc = 60. Under first-degree price discrimination with the same demand and MC curves, which output is chosen and what happens to the deadweight-loss triangle?

choices.A: Q = 30; the deadweight-loss triangle remains but becomes producer surplus.
choices.B: Q = 60; the deadweight-loss triangle disappears because all units with willingness to pay above MC are produced.
choices.C: Q = 60; the deadweight-loss triangle becomes consumer surplus.
choices.D: Q = 90; output exceeds the efficient level because the firm can charge every buyer differently.

questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination expands output to the efficient quantity, eliminating the deadweight loss from restricted output. A keeps ordinary monopoly quantity. C misallocates the former DWL; it is created as additional surplus captured by the firm. D incorrectly predicts overproduction beyond P = MC.

questions.question: Why is first-degree price discrimination allocatively efficient but often viewed as unfavorable to consumers?

choices.A: It produces all units with willingness to pay above MC, but transfers all consumer surplus to the seller.
choices.B: It restricts output below the monopoly level but gives consumers lower prices.
choices.C: It sets one uniform price equal to average cost.
choices.D: It creates deadweight loss by charging low-value consumers too little.

questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination is efficient because Q reaches the P = MC level. However, buyers receive no consumer surplus because each pays their maximum willingness to pay. B reverses the output effect. C describes average-cost pricing. D misunderstands the pricing rule.

questions.question: A car dealer knows each buyer's exact reservation price and can prevent resale. It negotiates a separate take-it-or-leave-it price with every buyer. Which model best applies?

choices.A: First-degree price discrimination, because each buyer is charged their maximum willingness to pay.
choices.B: Second-degree price discrimination, because buyers choose among quantity bundles.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination, because buyers are divided into observable groups only.
choices.D: Perfect competition, because negotiation eliminates market power.

questions.explanation: The key features are perfect information about individual willingness to pay and individualized prices, which define first-degree price discrimination. B involves self-selection among menus. C involves group-based pricing. D ignores the seller's market power and individualized extraction.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes first-degree price discrimination from ordinary monopoly pricing?

choices.A: First-degree price discrimination charges each unit according to willingness to pay and produces where P = MC; ordinary monopoly charges one price and produces where MR = MC.
choices.B: First-degree price discrimination charges one price and produces where MR = MC; ordinary monopoly charges each buyer a different price.
choices.C: Both produce the same output, but first-degree price discrimination has lower fixed cost.
choices.D: Ordinary monopoly eliminates consumer surplus, while first-degree price discrimination creates deadweight loss.

questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination extracts individual surplus and expands output to the efficient level. Ordinary monopoly restricts output using MR = MC under a single price. B reverses definitions. C ignores output differences. D reverses the welfare properties.

questions.question: Demand is P = 50 - Q and MC = 10. Compare ordinary monopoly with first-degree price discrimination. What is the change in total surplus when the firm switches to first-degree price discrimination?

choices.A: Total surplus increases by 200.
choices.B: Total surplus decreases by 200.
choices.C: Total surplus is unchanged, but consumer surplus falls to zero.
choices.D: Total surplus increases by 800.

questions.explanation: Ordinary monopoly: MR = 50 - 2Q = 10 gives Qm = 20 and Pm = 30. Efficient output is Qc = 40. The monopoly DWL is 0.5(40 - 20)(30 - 10) = 200. First-degree price discrimination eliminates this DWL, so total surplus rises by 200. B reverses the sign. C ignores DWL elimination. D confuses total efficient surplus with the change.

questions.question: A theme park sells ride tickets with a two-block price schedule: the first 10 rides cost $4 each and additional rides cost $2 each. A visitor's marginal willingness to pay for rides is P = 12 - Q. Ignoring income effects, how many rides will the visitor buy?

choices.A: 8 rides
choices.B: 10 rides
choices.C: 12 rides
choices.D: 14 rides

questions.explanation: For the first block, price is $4, and the visitor would buy until 12 - Q = 4, or Q = 8, but the lower second-block price begins only after 10 rides. The 10th ride has willingness to pay $2, equal to the second-block price threshold issue, so the visitor reaches 10 but buys no additional rides because for Q > 10, 12 - Q < 2. A ignores the bundle threshold. C treats the $0 marginal point incorrectly. D buys units with willingness to pay below price.

questions.question: A gym offers a two-part plan: a fixed monthly fee F plus a per-visit price of $5. A student's monthly demand for visits is P = 25 - Q. Marginal cost is $5. What fixed fee extracts all consumer surplus while keeping the student willing to join?
questions.explanation: With per-visit price equal to MC = $5, the student chooses Q = 20. Consumer surplus is 0.5(25 - 5)(20) = 200, so the maximum fixed fee is $200. A halves the surplus incorrectly. C uses only the price difference times half the wrong quantity. D uses Q = 10 instead of Q = 20.

questions.question: A phone company offers a quantity discount: $30 per GB for the first 2 GB and $10 per GB after that. If marginal cost of data falls from $8 to $4, what is the likely effect on the optimal second-block price, holding demand types fixed?

choices.A: The second-block price tends to fall because serving extra usage becomes less costly, making larger bundles more attractive for surplus extraction.
choices.B: The second-block price tends to rise because lower marginal cost requires a higher markup.
choices.C: The second-block price must stay at $10 because second-degree discrimination never responds to cost.
choices.D: The second-block price becomes zero because marginal cost is lower.

questions.explanation: Lower marginal cost makes additional units cheaper to provide, so the seller can profitably encourage more usage through lower marginal prices while extracting surplus through fixed or inframarginal charges. B reverses the cost effect. C falsely treats the menu as cost-independent. D assumes price must fall all the way to zero.

questions.question: A software firm sells Basic and Pro plans. High-demand users value extra features more than low-demand users. If the difference between high-demand and low-demand users' willingness to pay increases, what happens to the firm's incentive to distort the Basic plan downward?

choices.A: It increases because making Basic less attractive helps induce high-demand users to self-select into Pro.
choices.B: It decreases because larger differences make all incentive constraints irrelevant.
choices.C: It disappears because second-degree price discrimination becomes first-degree price discrimination.
choices.D: It causes the firm to price Basic below marginal cost for all users.

questions.explanation: In second-degree price discrimination, menus are designed for self-selection. When high types value quality much more, the firm may make the low-end option less attractive to reduce high types' temptation to choose it. B overstates separation. C confuses self-selection with perfect information. D is not generally implied.

questions.question: A block-pricing graph shows a consumer's downward-sloping demand for electricity and a marginal price that falls after 500 kWh. What graph feature explains why usage jumps at the block threshold for some consumers?

choices.A: The marginal price schedule has a kink, so once the consumer reaches the cheaper block, additional units are compared with a lower marginal price.
choices.B: The demand curve becomes horizontal after 500 kWh.
choices.C: Average cost must rise at exactly 500 kWh.
choices.D: Consumer surplus is impossible under block pricing.

questions.explanation: A declining block tariff creates a kink in the budget/payment schedule. Consumers compare marginal willingness to pay with the lower marginal price after the threshold. B invents a demand change. C adds an unsupported cost claim. D is false.

questions.question: In a two-part tariff diagram, the per-unit price equals MC, and the fixed fee equals the area under demand and above MC up to the chosen quantity. What does the fixed fee represent?

choices.A: Extracted consumer surplus from access to the product.
choices.B: Deadweight loss from pricing above MC.
choices.C: Marginal cost of the last unit.
choices.D: The slope of the budget constraint.

questions.explanation: When the usage price equals MC, the fixed fee can extract the consumer surplus generated by efficient usage. B is wrong because P = MC creates no usage distortion. C confuses fixed and marginal components. D refers to the per-unit price, not the fixed fee.

questions.question: Why does second-degree price discrimination not require the seller to know each buyer's exact type?

choices.A: Because buyers reveal information through their choices among quantity or quality packages.
choices.B: Because all buyers are charged their exact willingness to pay.
choices.C: Because resale is always impossible in competitive markets.
choices.D: Because the seller sets one uniform price for all units.

questions.explanation: Second-degree price discrimination uses self-selection: different types choose different packages. B describes first-degree discrimination. C is neither sufficient nor the defining feature. D describes uniform pricing.

questions.question: A cloud storage company offers 100 GB for $5 per month, 1 TB for $12 per month, and 5 TB for $30 per month. It does not know each user's exact demand, but users choose their preferred plan. Which model best applies?

choices.A: Second-degree price discrimination through a menu of quantity plans.
choices.B: First-degree price discrimination because each user pays their maximum willingness to pay.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination because each user is assigned a group by age.
choices.D: Perfect competition because users choose voluntarily.

questions.explanation: The firm offers a menu of quantities and lets users self-select, which is second-degree price discrimination. B would require individualized willingness-to-pay information. C would require observable group pricing. D confuses voluntary choice with competitive pricing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes second-degree from third-degree price discrimination?

choices.A: Second-degree uses self-selection among menus; third-degree charges different prices to observable groups.
choices.B: Second-degree charges each individual their exact willingness to pay; third-degree uses quantity discounts.
choices.C: Second-degree requires no market power; third-degree requires perfect competition.
choices.D: Second-degree always eliminates deadweight loss; third-degree always increases it.

questions.explanation: Second-degree discrimination is menu-based and induces self-selection. Third-degree discrimination segments buyers into observable groups. B confuses first-, second-, and third-degree discrimination. C is false because price discrimination requires market power. D makes unsupported welfare claims.

questions.question: A two-part tariff charges a per-unit price equal to MC = $4. A representative consumer has demand P = 24 - Q. What fixed fee extracts all consumer surplus, and what is total surplus from trade?

choices.A: Fixed fee = $200; total surplus = $200.
choices.B: Fixed fee = $100; total surplus = $200.
choices.C: Fixed fee = $200; total surplus = $100.
choices.D: Fixed fee = $80; total surplus = $160.

questions.explanation: With per-unit price equal to MC, Q = 20. Total surplus is the triangle under demand above MC: 0.5(24 - 4)(20) = 200. The maximum fixed fee extracts exactly that consumer surplus, so F = 200. B halves the fee. C halves total surplus. D uses an incorrect quantity.

questions.question: A monopolist sells to students and adults with inverse demands Ps = 60 - Qs and Pa = 100 - Qa. Marginal cost is constant at 20. If resale is impossible, what quantities and prices maximize profit?
questions.explanation: Set MR equal to MC in each market. MRs = 60 - 2Qs = 20 gives Qs = 20 and Ps = 40. MRa = 100 - 2Qa = 20 gives Qa = 40 and Pa = 60. B uses P = MC. C sets MR = 0. D gets quantities but charges MC.

questions.question: A publisher sells an ebook in two countries. Demand in country 1 is P1 = 80 - Q1; demand in country 2 is P2 = 50 - 0.5Q2. Marginal cost is 20. What are the optimal prices?
questions.explanation: MR1 = 80 - 2Q1 = 20 gives Q1 = 30 and P1 = 50. MR2 = 50 - Q2 = 20 gives Q2 = 30 and P2 = 35. B uses marginal-cost pricing. C uses revenue-maximizing quantities. D uses incorrect MR slopes.

questions.question: A firm price discriminates across two groups with the same MC. Group A demand becomes more elastic while Group B demand is unchanged. What happens to the optimal relative price in Group A?

choices.A: It falls relative to Group B because the Lerner markup is smaller when demand is more elastic.
choices.B: It rises relative to Group B because elastic consumers buy more when prices are high.
choices.C: It becomes equal to Group B's price because all discriminatory pricing requires equal prices.
choices.D: It falls to marginal cost while Group B price becomes infinite.

questions.explanation: Third-degree price discrimination charges lower markups to more elastic groups. The mechanism is the Lerner rule applied separately by market. B reverses elasticity logic. C denies price discrimination. D exaggerates the effect.

questions.question: A monopolist sells in two separated markets. Market 1 demand is unchanged. Market 2 demand shifts upward, raising its MR curve at every quantity. Marginal cost is constant and common. What happens in Market 2?

choices.A: Quantity in Market 2 rises because the firm expands until the higher MR again equals common MC.
choices.B: Quantity in Market 2 falls because higher demand allows more restriction.
choices.C: Quantity in Market 2 is unchanged because prices must differ but quantities must be equal.
choices.D: Market 2 price must fall to marginal cost.

questions.explanation: With separable markets and constant common MC, an upward shift in Market 2's MR leads the firm to increase Market 2 output until MR2 = MC again. B reverses the MR effect. C invents an equal-quantity condition. D applies competitive pricing.

questions.question: A third-degree price discrimination graph has separate demand and MR curves for seniors and non-seniors. At the chosen quantities, what must be equal across the two markets?

choices.A: Prices must be equal across the two markets.
choices.B: Marginal revenues must be equal to each other and to marginal cost.
choices.C: Quantities must be equal across the two markets.
choices.D: Consumer surpluses must be equal across the two markets.

questions.explanation: Profit maximization allocates sales so MR in each market equals common MC. Prices can differ because demand elasticities differ. A denies discrimination. C and D are not optimization conditions.

questions.question: On separate demand diagrams, the business traveler demand curve is steeper than the tourist demand curve at their chosen quantities. Which graphical implication is most likely under third-degree price discrimination?

choices.A: Business travelers are charged a higher price because their demand is less elastic.
choices.B: Tourists are charged a higher price because their demand curve is flatter.
choices.C: Both groups are charged the same price because MC is common.
choices.D: The group with steeper demand must receive a price below MC.

questions.explanation: A steeper demand curve at the relevant point usually means less elastic demand, which supports a higher markup. B reverses the elasticity logic. C ignores discriminatory pricing. D contradicts profit maximization with positive market power.

questions.question: Why must resale be difficult for third-degree price discrimination to work?

choices.A: Otherwise low-price buyers could resell to high-price buyers, undermining separate market prices.
choices.B: Otherwise the firm could not calculate marginal cost.
choices.C: Otherwise all consumers would have identical elasticities.
choices.D: Otherwise demand curves would slope upward.

questions.explanation: Price discrimination requires market separation. If resale is easy, arbitrage erodes price differences. B is unrelated. C is false because resale does not determine elasticity. D is unrelated to arbitrage.

questions.question: A movie theater charges students $9 and adults $14 for the same film. IDs are checked and tickets cannot be resold. Which model best applies, and what condition should hold at the optimum?

choices.A: Third-degree price discrimination; MR_student = MR_adult = MC.
choices.B: Second-degree price discrimination; students self-select by buying larger bundles.
choices.C: First-degree price discrimination; each buyer pays exactly their willingness to pay.
choices.D: Perfect competition; P_student = P_adult = MC.

questions.explanation: The theater separates observable groups and prevents resale, so third-degree price discrimination applies. Profit maximization requires equal marginal revenue across groups and equal to MC. B is menu-based pricing. C requires individualized prices. D ignores market power and group pricing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes third-degree price discrimination from peak-load pricing?

choices.A: Third-degree price discrimination separates consumer groups by elasticity; peak-load pricing charges different prices across time periods when demand or capacity costs differ.
choices.B: Third-degree pricing always changes marginal cost; peak-load pricing always requires identical demand curves.
choices.C: Third-degree pricing is based on quantity blocks; peak-load pricing charges each buyer their maximum willingness to pay.
choices.D: They are identical because both involve more than one price.

questions.explanation: Third-degree discrimination segments consumers; peak-load pricing varies by time and capacity scarcity. B is false. C confuses second- and first-degree discrimination with peak-load pricing. D ignores the different economic mechanisms.

questions.question: A firm sells in two separated markets with demands P1 = 100 - Q1 and P2 = 80 - Q2. MC = 20. Under third-degree price discrimination, what is total deadweight loss relative to marginal-cost pricing in both markets?
questions.explanation: Market 1: MR1 = 100 - 2Q1 = 20 gives Q1m = 40, P1m = 60; Q1c = 80; DWL1 = 0.5(40)(40) = 800. Market 2: MR2 = 80 - 2Q2 = 20 gives Q2m = 30, P2m = 50; Q2c = 60; DWL2 = 0.5(30)(30) = 450. Total DWL = 1,250. Since 1,250 is not listed, this item should be revised before use; the closest listed value C is not exactly correct.

questions.question: A club can charge a fixed membership fee F and a per-visit price p. A representative member has demand P = 30 - Q and the club's marginal cost is 6. If all members are identical, what two-part tariff maximizes profit while inducing efficient usage?
questions.explanation: Set the per-unit price equal to MC: p = 6, so Q = 24. Consumer surplus at p = 6 is 0.5(30 - 6)(24) = 288, which can be extracted as the fixed fee. B uses the uniform monopoly price. C halves the surplus incorrectly. D sets the choke price and sells no visits.

questions.question: A streaming platform offers a fixed subscription fee plus a per-movie rental price. A user's monthly demand is P = 20 - 0.5Q and MC = 4. For identical users, what per-movie price and fixed fee extract all surplus efficiently?
questions.explanation: Set p = MC = 4. Demand gives 20 - 0.5Q = 4, so Q = 32. Consumer surplus is 0.5(20 - 4)(32) = 256. A uses a monopoly-like per-unit price. C halves the surplus. D prices at the choke price.

questions.question: A gym uses a two-part tariff for identical consumers with demand P = 40 - Q. Marginal cost rises from 10 to 20. What happens to the efficient per-visit price and maximum fixed fee?

choices.A: Per-visit price rises from 10 to 20, and the fixed fee falls from 450 to 200 because consumer surplus at marginal-cost pricing shrinks.
choices.B: Per-visit price stays at 10, and fixed fee rises because costs are higher.
choices.C: Per-visit price rises from 10 to 20, and fixed fee rises from 450 to 800.
choices.D: Per-visit price falls because higher MC reduces quantity demanded.

questions.explanation: With p = MC = 10, Q = 30 and CS = 0.5(30)(30) = 450. With p = 20, Q = 20 and CS = 0.5(20)(20) = 200. Higher MC raises the usage price and lowers extractable surplus. B ignores MC pricing. C reverses the fixed fee effect. D reverses the usage price effect.

questions.question: A two-part tariff seller faces identical consumers. Demand shifts upward from P = 30 - Q to P = 40 - Q, while MC remains 10. What happens to the optimal fixed fee and usage quantity?

choices.A: Quantity rises from 20 to 30 and the fixed fee rises from 200 to 450 because consumer surplus at p = MC increases.
choices.B: Quantity stays at 20 and the fixed fee rises from 200 to 400 because only willingness to pay changes.
choices.C: Quantity rises from 10 to 15 and the fixed fee falls because demand is higher.
choices.D: Quantity falls because a higher fixed fee discourages all usage after entry.

questions.explanation: The per-unit price remains p = MC = 10. Initially Q = 20 and CS = 0.5(20)(20) = 200. After demand rises, Q = 30 and CS = 0.5(30)(30) = 450. B ignores the quantity effect. C uses monopoly quantities. D confuses the entry fee with marginal usage price after joining.

questions.question: On a two-part tariff diagram for identical consumers, the usage price is set equal to MC. What does the area between demand and MC up to the chosen quantity represent?

choices.A: The maximum fixed fee the firm can charge without making the consumer decline membership.
choices.B: Deadweight loss caused by the two-part tariff.
choices.C: The marginal cost of access.
choices.D: The monopolist's lost revenue from setting p = MC.

questions.explanation: At p = MC, usage is efficient and the consumer surplus triangle can be extracted through the fixed fee. B is wrong because efficient usage creates no DWL. C confuses fixed access pricing with marginal cost. D ignores that the fixed fee recovers surplus.

questions.question: A two-part tariff has a high fixed fee and a low per-unit price. On the consumer's graph, how does the fixed fee appear relative to the marginal consumption decision?

choices.A: It shifts total surplus division but does not change the marginal condition P = p for usage after the consumer joins.
choices.B: It changes the slope of the demand curve.
choices.C: It raises marginal cost for every unit consumed.
choices.D: It makes the consumer choose the quantity where total expenditure is minimized.

questions.explanation: The fixed fee affects participation, not the marginal price of additional units after joining. Usage depends on the per-unit price. B and C incorrectly make the fixed fee marginal. D is not the consumer's choice rule.

questions.question: Why can a two-part tariff be more profitable than a single monopoly price when consumers are identical?

choices.A: It can set the usage price at marginal cost to maximize total surplus, then extract that surplus with the fixed fee.
choices.B: It creates deadweight loss and converts that deadweight loss into profit.
choices.C: It forces marginal cost to fall below zero.
choices.D: It prevents consumers from responding to marginal prices.

questions.explanation: A two-part tariff separates efficient usage pricing from surplus extraction. The firm can avoid the output restriction of uniform monopoly pricing while using the access fee to capture surplus. B is wrong because deadweight loss is not captured by anyone. C is impossible from pricing alone. D is false because consumers still respond to the per-unit price.

questions.question: A warehouse store charges $80 per year for membership and then sells groceries at low per-unit markups close to marginal cost. Which model best explains this pricing strategy?

choices.A: Two-part tariff: an access fee plus low usage prices.
choices.B: First-degree price discrimination: each item is priced at each shopper's willingness to pay.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination: different observable groups face different grocery prices.
choices.D: Peak-load pricing: prices differ only by time of day.

questions.explanation: The membership fee plus low per-unit price is a two-part tariff. B requires individualized willingness-to-pay pricing. C requires group-specific prices. D requires time-based scarcity pricing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a two-part tariff from a standard uniform monopoly price?

choices.A: A two-part tariff uses both a fixed fee and a per-unit price; a uniform monopoly price uses a single per-unit price for all units.
choices.B: A two-part tariff always charges every consumer a different personalized price; a uniform monopoly price charges a fixed fee only.
choices.C: A two-part tariff cannot create profit; a uniform monopoly price always extracts all surplus.
choices.D: A two-part tariff requires P = ATC; a uniform monopoly price requires P = MC.

questions.explanation: The defining feature of a two-part tariff is the combination of an access fee and a usage price. B confuses it with first-degree price discrimination. C reverses the surplus-extraction logic. D confuses tariff design with regulatory pricing rules.

questions.question: A consumer has demand P = 60 - Q and MC = 20. Compare a uniform monopoly price with an efficient two-part tariff for identical consumers. By how much does total surplus increase under the two-part tariff?
questions.explanation: Uniform monopoly: MR = 60 - 2Q = 20 gives Qm = 20 and Pm = 40. Efficient quantity under two-part tariff with p = MC is Qc = 40. The increase in total surplus equals the eliminated DWL: 0.5(40 - 20)(40 - 20) = 200. B uses the full rectangle. C and D overstate the welfare gain.
